speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing survivor, Abraham Melezin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --notes because I would like to give you a sequence of events the way I remember because it involves many people. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Look--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in that gates, in the-- it is covered, with an arc-- arch over it. And I tried, you know, to get closer to him and to see how I can wrench out a revolver. After all, you know, I had army behind me, and so on. So I knew some tricks, you know, know how to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] careful. He kept me-- when I wanted to come to him, he said, no. Stand there. And, and there was another guy who went on the third floor to catch other Jews, you know, to bring them down. At that moment, I was standing, you know, in that gate-- came a column of Jews, led by a very well-known criminal and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish criminal, a Pole. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they saw me. And they saw the other guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they broke the column. They ran to me. And the other guy, you know, the Lithuanian guy, became confused. So it was-- nevermind what happened to him, you know. But they liberated me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, so their leader didn't want to take me into camp. So-- until now, there were a great many Jews from the-- from the upper part of Kalvaliska Street, whom I know. And now, professor, professor, what has happened to you, and so on. Come in. Then one of them said, look, if you have money, give them money right now. So I had some money notes. So I said, look, let me work in your-- in your account. And I said, you won't be sorry, you know. And I started to work there, in the military barracks, on Snipiskes there. And I worked there all the time, always coming up with a column. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and then the rumors started to circulate that we will be put in the ghetto. Let me tell one thing. In-between, in Vilna, were terrible things. They used to catch Jews from streets, take them to the prison-- Lukiszki-- from there to the suburban place, Ponary, or, in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], and shoot them. And they took away one of my good friends, Jacob Gordon. He had a timber business, you know. And he disappeared. Then another somebody disappeared-- and just disappeared, it seemed. The people were-- and started that-- supposedly, they went somewhere to work. But then, once I met the-- my Wanda Rewenska, who said, they didn't disappear. They were all shot in Ponary, all shot. There is, she said, a big ditch. And every day there is constant, incessant shooting. You should be careful. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the Vilna Ghetto before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Dr. Melezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] It was-- we worked there. And I came over as a [NON-ENGLISH]-- very interesting. If you work for Wehrmacht, it means for the army-- and by and large, at that time they were not so dehumanized and so on-- victorious. They were very pleasant people. Then came the Einsatzgruppen, the SS men. So the sergeant-- I work as an interpreter there. So I told them, now be careful. The [GERMAN] has come. [GERMAN]-- this is how he would talk about them. Then they disappeared, you know. They moved out. This was very hard. There were beatings. And we had to bribe everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You watched-- did you see some of the beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we worked there, you know, in the military barracks building, rebuilding various things. And there was-- when the commander of the Einsatzgruppen, who was there-- so it was all the time, terrible beatings. They beat for anything, what-- you didn't stand properly, you were hit. You didn't run too fast, you were hit, you know. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this while you were already in the Vilna Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not yet in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is summer-- it was in August, through August. In-- and then, it started to rumor that they'll organize ghetto. I remember I argued with her brother-- with Rachela's brother, what will be better, to be all together in the ghetto or be separated? By that time, I was called once to-- for work. If I say called for work, despite the [NON-ENGLISH], the commander, rather. And once I was unloading some kind of food for Germans. And-- but once, they took us to [NON-ENGLISH], where there was a company of those SS men. And we came up to this place-- to the very first thing that I saw-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] several graves, fresh graves, you know-- scent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So what they did, you know, they made out of us chain and were driving us. Because they saw-- they suspected that there might be mines. Because before the-- before they came there, were these sort of garrisons. They suspect there are-- so instead of looking for mines themselves, they, you know, make a human chain and tried to do it. And, and I remember Zelig, telling me, this is our last day. So I told him, look, there are two possibilities. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have a watch. I'll give the [NON-ENGLISH], the German-- I'll grab the watch. And I'll start a run to-- through the forest, the respite.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you follow me. So you'll go through the forest. But he himself knew, because he was in the same-- he was in the timber business. So he knew very well, you know. He said, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that moment, came out a representative-- higher officer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, called out another one, and told me, don't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoot anybody. Don't try. Behave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, they tracked everything, and so on. There was a German, unexploded aerial bomb. So they used to-- it was about 25 kilograms, you know. So we had to carry it and drop it and stand there 10 or 15 seconds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, the bomb was bad, didn't, didn't explode. Toward the end of the day, they started to beat us, but let us go. So I returned-- all right. It was in September-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember Saturday, September-- no, it was Friday, September the 5th. At that time, they turned us from one barracks to another group of barracks-- couple days before. And we were gathered. And we found, you know, there were some Russian POWs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they treated them terribly. And to me came up one, a Jew. He says, how can-- I said, because they'll, they'll kill us. What you can do, you know, to drop your clothes somehow and go out. And I didn't know what happen to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Saturday morning, we came to work in that [NON-ENGLISH] Barracks. Sunday, we were diverted to the center of the city, the whole column. What to do? And I saw that we are-- started to finish up fences-- there now, that the other commanders already put up fences around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, there were Aktions. They cleaned out the old part of Jewish city. It was, you know, it was going back to 16th century-- very interesting in architecture, very interesting in how it grew. I have here painting, aquarelle, which explains it, how it grew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a German said, you have to finish up before noon because this is the ghetto. And you-- you'll stay here. So we said, the ghetto? So what I did-- the first thing, I, I-- you know, as a geographer, I, I knew that part would be Vilna. So 1, 2, 3-- and I was outside. I escaped and ran home. And there was already the [NON-ENGLISH]. It means the order. Whatever we can take, take it. And within two hours, move out. So, I came and found Rachela and Sula. And everybody cried. And Iuzia, that nanny that's ours, cried. So I had a very good backpack-- and two of them. Because we used to go, you know, summers as geographers. And we packed whatever it is. And then I started to give some, you know, explanations to Iuzia-- what to do with the rest of the things, you know. This you will sell. This we will bring, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the ghetto. And we entered ghetto late afternoon. And I remember it was through the gate-- they finished up already, the gates and the fences. It was the gate on Rudnicka Street. And right to the left was the Shavelska Street. And I was-- jumped in the first house, which they have emptied before and people took away to Lukiszki and Ponary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, Rachela came in with me-- and Dora and Zelig. So what happened-- we had-- we had one room with one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided that in the bed will sleep Sula with the child, who is Zarek. Rachela slept somewhere on the floor. Dora and her two children slept on the other place on the floor. I was in the rocker.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was Sunday. So we tried to organize, you know, ourselves. But by the middle of the day, another order came. Those who worked in proper places, go to work. So I-- we went to work. I went to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I returned. And we brought-- you see, we brought along some food. So the moment, we even didn't think. But there was such a chaos, you know, like a Tower of Babel, you know, people going here and there. Children were lost, parents separated. It is hard to-- from the city, they used to-- some carts used to bring cripples, sick. Where to put-- they used to throw them right on the floor. Then came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, there was organized-- I don't want to talk about it. I can tell only one thing, that the Judenrat was leftist whereas the police was Betarist.

speaker: interviewer
text: The police of the ghetto were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish police. And there were-- great many of my students from both schools, from all the schools, from the private school where I used to teach and from former [NON-ENGLISH] school, Tarbut. They start to work. And then, when we went to work, so, in a day or two-- so the first days, you know, we were so confused that I even don't remember exactly. But I know that every day, we used to go to work. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one commandant doing work was taken away to the prison Lukiszki and later to Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rumor started that we need passes. So, when I came-- when we came to that work to that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] barracks. So I asked the, the Bauleiter-- it means one who-- overseer-- I said, should we have passes? We need passes? So he said, I-- we have no time to do passes. If you-- I have no paper. If you will bring me ready, tight passes, I do. I like the way they work, I put stamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Luckily enough, on the way back I saw the janitor from the Department of Geography, who, who had very close relations on a-- on account of different things. So I said, look, tell Mrs. Rewenska that I will come tomorrow. And I will type passes in German. It was the next day, when the count went. I asked the-- a young-- bribed the, the, the leader, that criminal. You know, he had black [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mustache, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which he colored even blacker, you know. So we called him Mustacher, Black Mustacher. So, we bribed him. And when we were on the corner of Zawalna and [NON-ENGLISH], a side street, where Mrs. Rewenska had an apartment. I went out, came there, and started to type. And I made sure I told them the way back. I knew where we were, go through the same route. Don't go through Vilenska Street. Go through Zawalna Street. Because you could go there either through Vilenska Street or through Zawalna Street, through different gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I typed out about 350 passes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He came there. And he told me, look, I want more money. So, I-- we made, you know, a collection. And he got more money. He came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said, look, let's sit down. And I'll write in, by hand, your names. I remember, they kissed my hands. They embraced me. They want to give me money. But I had money, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I said, look, we know-- we don't-- now. When we see what happens-- don't depart from your money so easily. So I did it-- what, I work all day? Believe me, I said, I work-- you worked in the barracks much harder, were beaten, while I was sitting in the good chair. And I had a very good lunch and dinner, you know. So, I made out 330 or so or 350, I don't remember. No, some-- I had some reserves left-- 20 passes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we went there. And again, I told them to prepare money. We bribed the men from the, the secretariat. And we got 350 white passes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I worked through September. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the end of September, we were thrown out to another-- different barracks, closer to the river Vilnia on a street called [NON-ENGLISH]. These were barracks of artillery. There were barracks of, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, infantry.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time-- during these months, were there people in the ghetto who were starving? Were there people who were having difficulty in surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as I know, a Judenrat set up a public kitchen. And they used to bring in, from outside, allotted rations of bread and some vegetables, whatever or not. And I really don't know-- I really don't know how it was set up, or where it-- was it. But Sula and the child were in, in the ghetto, didn't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and didn't go out. But once in a while, Iuzia would come. And I would smuggle in-- into the ghetto, some food for the child and other food, too. At the beginning, it was quite easily done. And if they started to search-- so we would bribe the Lithuanian guards on the other side, you know. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Melezin, how long did you remain in the ghetto, you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I want you to know. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] On Wednesday, October the 1st, Yom Kippur--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that, Dr.--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came from work. I remember, I brought in milk. I brought in butter Iuzia gave me. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and suddenly, there was an alarm. Somebody ran near. I don't know who. The ghetto is surrounded by SS and Ukrainian Police. And in the next moment, you know, the Jewish policemen, get out! Get out! Get out! Everybody has to register at the gate, or whatever it is-- starting to be driven out from-- whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: How to get that? Take something. Even the-- you know, I just came in from work. And I'll be having this-- in those-- that months and now or three weeks make-- here is this piece, you know-- absolutely dissolved. But I got something of myself, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sula got something-- we took the-- first of all, you know for the child because in the evenings, it was warm. And we went out. Rachela went out. And then nearest the-- it was very close to the gate. This is-- was one of the houses very close to the exit street. And a couple Germans ran around, ordering us to get in fives, or whatever it is, fives or tens, I don't remember exactly. But I remember one thing. I was on the left flank. Sula was, then I, with Zarek, holding Zarek. Then there was somebody-- maybe it was Dora. It seems to me Dora and Zelig. And then Rachela was at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And two SS men came up, officers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my wife, who spoke beautiful German, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she started-- she told them, look, why we are here? My husband has a, a, a pass. Go and show them your pass. So I showed them the pass. So they gave him back-- they gave it back. She was like, why? He has to go to work there. And there's a whole commanders who have to go to work there. He said, nevermind, because we don't need commander-- he said. Yes, he's a-- she said, and I'll show you, too. He is a doctor. Ah, he's a doctor? Show diploma. So she had my PhD diploma. He took, you know. Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], Geography, or whatever or not, and so on. And then he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Are you from the Posen? Because Posen was once German part, you know, of Poland. And there, everybody speaks German. So she said, no. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stood there for maybe an hour. It became already dark. And then they ordered march out. I don't know how Rachela disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked around. Already, I was so confused, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to her, how I let myself get caught in such a manner? You know? What went through my head? And we went out. And here, Lithuanians started to beat us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I held Zarek. So he tried to hit the child. So I tried to save him. So since then, Zarek, who started to talk-- all the time, used to repeat-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, not Lithuanian-- he said, Lithuanian beat Daddy. Lithuanian-- and when I was already, you know, in Underground on the other side, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the men came up, why your son tells me that you-- a Lithuanian beat you? So I told him, look, one of the reasons why we left Vilna, was that I had some quarrel with a Lithuanian policeman. And he beat me up. And I beat him up back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, I looked around, and I saw that they are-- they are driving us toward Lukiszki. And they were driving us through all broad streets, not narrow, you know. So I said-- so, I tried to bribe him. I, I, I had some money. It seems I had some-- a dollar or two, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told him, what's the difference? You won't get there better. No, you kike and whatever, nothing. So it never worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was-- driven us to Lukiszki. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The women, they told me to give away my child to the-- to my wife, to Sula. And the women were put into-- under the roof. We were driven some kind of yard and spent the night there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I looked around. And, you know, and I saw the only-- the only one face which I saw was that of a neighbor of ours, on Snipiskes, near the other gardener, you know, the other farm. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, look, what to do? I have money. I said, you have money? What, what may I think? Try to bribe somebody? There's nobody here. Let's change the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]
